sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current blue square positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check horizontal passages for left and right squares

print("\nLeft square horizontal scan (row 51):")
print(f"  {ascii_lines[51][10:55]}")
print("\nRight square horizontal scan (row 56):")
print(f"  {ascii_lines[56][10:55]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
So the squares moved right:
- Left square: from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28 (moved 5 cols right)
- Right square: from cols 39-43 to cols 34-38 (moved 5 cols LEFT!)
Wait, the right square moved LEFT? That's strange. I pressed RIGHT but the right square moved left. Let me check this more carefully.
Actually, looking at the positions:
- Left square: cols 24-28 (was 19-23, moved +5)
- Right square: cols 34-38 (was 39-43, moved -5)
So the left square moved right and the right square moved left - they moved TOWARDS each other! This suggests that arrow keys move the squares towards the center, not in the direction of the arrow.
Wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me think about this differently. Maybe the squares move in the direction of the arrow but are constrained by walls. The left square moved right (towards the center passage), and the right square also moved right but... no, it moved left.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the squares move towards each other when I press RIGHT? That would be weird. Or maybe there's a different control scheme.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine. The left square was at cols 19-23 and moved to cols 24-28. The right square was at cols 39-43 and moved to cols 34-38. So:
- Left square: +5 cols (right)
- Right square: -5 cols (left)
They moved towards each other! This is interesting. Maybe RIGHT means "move squares towards each other horizontally" and LEFT means "move squares apart horizontally"?
Or maybe the squares always move towards the center of the maze? Let me test this hypothesis by pressing LEFT to see if they move apart.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test LEFT to see if squares move apart
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
